summary: Demis Hassabis discusses DeepMind's role as the AI engine room for Alphabet, the development of world models like GENIE, and the trajectory toward AGI. He emphasizes that true AGI requires an understanding of the physical world, consistency, and creativity, and predicts that AGI will usher in a new golden age of scientific discovery and human health.

principles.statement: True AGI must understand the physical world, not just abstract language or mathematics.
principles.rationale: To be truly general and to power applications like robotics or smart assistants, an AI system must comprehend intuitive physics and the physical context of the world around us.
principles.source_quote: for an AI to be truly general, to build AGI, we feel that the AGI system needs to understand the world around us and the physical world around us, not just the abstract world of languages or mathematics.

principles.statement: AI is the ultimate tool for accelerating scientific discovery.
principles.rationale: AI can solve complex problems that are intractable to humans, from protein folding to material design, ultimately ushering in a new renaissance in science and human health.
principles.source_quote: if we build AGI in the right way, it will be the ultimate tool for science.

principles.statement: True AGI requires the ability to make intuitive leaps and exhibit true creativity.
principles.rationale: Current AI systems can prove given concepts but cannot generate new conjectures or hypotheses on their own. A true AGI should be able to invent entirely new theories or aesthetically beautiful strategies, much like history's greatest human scientists and artists.
principles.source_quote: AI today, I would say, doesn't have true creativity in the sense that it can't come up with a new conjecture yet or new hypothesis.

frameworks.name: Hybrid to End-to-End Learning
frameworks.when_to_apply: When building AI for complex scientific domains (like biology or chemistry) where training data is insufficient for pure probabilistic learning.
steps: Build a hybrid model combining a probabilistic learning component (neural networks) with deterministic, handcrafted rules (physics/chemistry constraints).
Use the hybrid system to figure out solutions and generate insights.
Reverse engineer the handcrafted rules and upstream that knowledge into the learning component.
Transition to end-to-end learning directly from the data without the handcrafted constraints.
frameworks.source_quote: once you've figured out something using one of these hybrid systems, you then try and go back and reverse engineer what you've done and see if you can incorporate that learning, that information into the learning system.

mental_models.name: Reverse Engineering Intuitive Physics
mental_models.description: Viewing AI video and world generation not as traditional 3D rendering with programmed rules, but as a neural network deducing the laws of physics purely by observing massive amounts of video data.
mental_models.example: The GENIE model generating a consistent 3D room where a user paints a wall, looks away, and looks back to find the paint still there, all generated on the fly without a traditional physics engine.

mental_models.name: Android for Robotics
mental_models.description: Approaching robotics software as a generalized operating system layer that can be deployed across various hardware form factors, similar to how Android operates across different smartphones.
mental_models.example: Developing generalized vision-language-action models that can be installed on millions of different robots to help society, rather than just vertically integrating software for one specific robot design.

heuristics: What separates a great scientist from a good scientist is they're both technically very capable, of course, but the great scientist is more creative.
It's always better if you can do end to end learning and directly predict the thing that you're after from the data that you're given.

signature_quotes.text: If we build AGI in the right way, it will be the ultimate tool for science.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the primary motivation behind his career and the most important application of AI.

signature_quotes.text: For an AI to be truly general, to build AGI, we feel that the AGI system needs to understand the world around us and the physical world around us, not just the abstract world of languages or mathematics.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why DeepMind is building multimodal world models like GENIE.

signature_quotes.text: You often hear some of our competitors talk about... these modern systems that we have today are PhD intelligences. I think that's a nonsense.
signature_quotes.context: Critiquing the lack of consistency in current LLMs, which can perform at a PhD level in some areas but fail at high school math.

signature_quotes.text: I think we'll have AGI in the next 10 years... and I think that will usher in a new golden era of science, so a kind of new renaissance.
signature_quotes.context: Predicting the state of the world 10 years from now.

anti_patterns.description: Labeling current Large Language Models as 'PhD intelligences.'
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Current models lack consistency. They might have some PhD-level capabilities but can still fail at high school math or simple counting. True general intelligence requires consistent performance across the board.

anti_patterns.description: Relying purely on probabilistic learning when training data is scarce.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: In fields like biology and chemistry, there isn't enough data for the model to learn fundamental physical laws from scratch. Doing so wastes learning capacity; it is better to constrain the model with known rules of physics and chemistry.
